title: Analyzing Access Paths
breadcrumbs: Design Considerations for Performance and Scalability
text: Analyzing Access Paths Access path analysis plays an important role in performance optimization, because access paths affect database I/O processing. Analyzing access paths is part of the process of designing an optimal data model. A business activity gains access to its user view by activating components. A component can access the entities directly or it can access them indirectly by invoking other components. Access path analysis is a technique to establish the impact of a component on the system load by directly accessing the entities that represent the data. The purpose of access path analysis is to: Verify whether the modeled entities support the user views Identify potential processing bottlenecks caused by accessing the application's database Access path analysis can be performed at different stages of the system life cycle. It is particularly useful during the design stage when the major decisions are taken that determine whether an application model sufficiently supports a certain user view and whether performance bottlenecks are to be expected. To perform access path analysis, follow these steps for the components you decide to prototype: Choose the user views to analyze, based on the core business requirements and associated risks. For more information, see Identify and Prioritize User Views . Implement the data design for the selected user views by defining modeled entities, fields, keys, and relationships. Define frames-within-frames component designs to establish the access paths. For more information, see Frames-Within-Frames Design . Analyze the access paths based on estimated data volumes to identify those that have the highest access stress rates. You can use the resulting access profile and statistics to adjust your data design before testing, or to identify the components to prototype and test first. For more information, see Component Entities and Paths and Access Profiles and Statistics . Create prototype components and test the data design with respect to benchmarking and providing contingencies for the risks. Refine the modeled objects. For more information, see Refining the Modeled Objects . Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
